1.synthesis: Paul describes the depth of the human plight: "you were dead in the trespasses and sins in which you once walked," following "the prince of the power of the air," "by nature children of wrath" (vv.1–3). Then the great reversal: "But God, being rich in mercy, because of the great love with which he loved us… made us alive together with Christ… and raised us up with him and seated us with him in the heavenly places" (vv.4–6). The point is salvation by sheer grace: "by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast" (vv.8–9). For we are "his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works" (v.10). The dead are raised by mercy alone.
1.historical_context: "Dead in… trespasses and sins" (v.1) is the Jewish covenantal category of exclusion from God's life-giving presence, not biological death — the Gentile readers had no covenant standing. "The prince of the power of the air" (v.2) reflects Second Temple cosmology, which located hostile spiritual powers in the lower atmosphere. The pivotal "But God" (v.4) follows the rhetorical pattern of Jewish lament — hopeless plight answered by divine rescue — and "rich in mercy" echoes God's self-revelation as abounding in steadfast love (Exodus 34:6). The cluster of syn- ("together with") verbs (vv.5–6) claims believers already share Christ's resurrection life, and "workmanship" (poiēma, v.10) is new-creation language (cf. 2 Corinthians 5:17).
1.literary_structure: The passage is built on a stark before/after contrast hinging on the emphatic "But God" of v.4: the depths of vv.1–3 (dead, enslaved, condemned) answered by the heights of vv.4–7 (alive, raised, enthroned). The syn- verbs — made alive with, raised with, seated with Christ (vv.5–6) — apply the exaltation of Ephesians 1:20 to believers, an inaugurated eschatology. The grace-through-faith summary (vv.8–9) is the doctrinal core, and v.10 rebalances grace and works: saved not by works but for them.
1.theological_application: The gospel here is at its purest: spiritually dead people cannot cooperate in their own resurrection, so salvation is entirely God's gift, "not a result of works, so that no one may boast" (v.9). This humbles every claim of merit and grounds assurance not in our doing but in God's mercy and love (v.4). Yet grace is not opposed to good works; it produces them — we are "created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared beforehand" (v.10). The Christian life is the outflow, not the cause, of salvation: rescued by grace, we walk in the works God appointed.
1.christology: The believer's new life is wholly bound to Christ's: to be "made alive together with Christ" and "raised" and "seated" with him (vv.5–6) means that union with the risen and enthroned Lord is the source of all spiritual life — only the One who himself rose can raise the dead. His resurrection and exaltation (Ephesians 1:20) are not his alone but shared with his people. And the new creation "in Christ Jesus" (v.10) restores the image of God marred by the fall, refitting humanity for the good works for which it was made.

11.pericope_label: Jews and Gentiles United in Christ
11.synthesis: Paul recalls the Gentiles' former alienation: "separated from Christ, alienated from the commonwealth of Israel… having no hope and without God in the world" (vv.11–12). "But now in Christ Jesus you who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ" (v.13). For "he himself is our peace," who broke down "the dividing wall of hostility" by abolishing in his flesh "the law of commandments expressed in ordinances," creating "in himself one new man in place of the two" and reconciling both to God "in one body through the cross" (vv.14–16). Now both have "access in one Spirit to the Father" (v.18); the Gentiles are "fellow citizens with the saints" and members of God's household (v.19), a "holy temple" being built into "a dwelling place for God by the Spirit" (vv.21–22).
11.historical_context: "Uncircumcision" (v.11) was a term of Jewish contempt, and the "covenants of promise" (v.12, deliberately plural) named the Abrahamic, Mosaic, and Davidic covenants from which Gentiles were excluded. "Far" and "near" (v.13, v.17) were rabbinic categories for Gentile and Jew, drawn from Isaiah 57:19. The "dividing wall" (v.14) evokes the temple barrier (the soreg) that warned Gentiles away on pain of death, and behind it the Torah's boundary-marking function — the food, purity, and circumcision rules that kept Jew and Gentile apart. "Fellow citizens" (v.19) is Roman civic language (politeia), elevating the former "resident aliens" to full membership and family standing.
11.literary_structure: This passage parallels vv.1–10: there a vertical reconciliation (dead sinners made alive to God), here a horizontal one (estranged peoples made one), both pivoting on a "but now" (v.13) and accomplished by the cross. The structure moves from the Gentiles' past plight (vv.11–12) through Christ's peace-making work (vv.13–18) to the new community (vv.19–22), shifting metaphors from citizenship to household to temple. The repeated "one" — one new man, one body, one Spirit (vv.15–18) — drives the theme of unity, and the temple imagery (v.21) fulfills the hope of God dwelling among his people.
11.theological_application: The cross reconciles not only sinners to God but enemies to one another: in Christ the deepest human division — Jew and Gentile — is healed, making "one new man" (v.15). This is a stern word to every church tempted to let ethnic, social, or cultural barriers divide it; such walls were torn down at Calvary. The Gentile reader is moved from "no hope and without God" (v.12) to full citizenship and family membership (v.19) — sheer grace to outsiders. And the church is God's new temple (vv.21–22): his presence now dwells not in a building but in a reconciled people.
11.christology: Christ is the peacemaker who is himself "our peace" (v.14): by his flesh and blood he abolished the enmity, fulfilling the covenants the Gentiles lacked and making one body of the two. The reconciliation is "through the cross" (v.16), which puts to death not only sin's penalty but the hostility between peoples. Through him both Jew and Gentile have "access in one Spirit to the Father" (v.18), a Trinitarian shape to salvation. And Christ is the "cornerstone" (v.20) whose precise placement aligns the whole structure — the church grows into a holy temple only as it is joined to him.
